patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 46-year-old female accounting clerk. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “My eyes and mouth have been so dry lately, it’s getting hard to get through the day.” Early in the visit, volunteer: gritty “sand” eyes worse in evenings, constant dry mouth with frequent sipping, trouble swallowing dry foods without water, more cavities despite brushing, chapped lips and dry throat. Do not volunteer hoarse voice, reflux at night, reduced taste, dry/itchy skin, or fingers turning pale/numb in cold unless specifically asked. Be hesitant/embarrassed about sexual discomfort and avoid labeling mood as “depression,” but admit feeling down/irritable if gently asked. Express misconceptions: allergies, dehydration, stress. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and open up more. Ask about costs/insurance for drops, dental work, labs, and missed work.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of Sjogren syndrome as the working diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "Sjogren syndrome" and describes it as an "autoimmune" condition.
Elicits key sicca symptoms (dry eyes and dry mouth) and symptom duration/severity.

Asks about red-flag/complication symptoms requiring escalation (eye pain/photophobia/vision change suggesting keratitis/uveitis; parotid swelling; dysphagia; systemic symptoms such as fevers, weight loss, night sweats; neuropathy).
Reviews relevant associated autoimmune features (arthralgias/arthritis, Raynaud phenomenon, rash, fatigue) and past history of autoimmune disease.

Assesses medication and non-autoimmune causes of sicca (anticholinergics, antihistamines, antidepressants, diuretics; dehydration; diabetes; thyroid disease; hepatitis C; HIV; sarcoidosis; prior head/neck radiation).
Documents a reasonable differential diagnosis including medication-induced sicca and other autoimmune/connective tissue disease (e.g., SLE/RA) or IgG4-related disease.

Communicates next-step evaluation at a high level (e.g., autoantibodies such as anti-SSA/Ro, anti-SSB/La; inflammatory markers; objective dryness testing; dental/ophthalmology evaluation) without requiring specific test ordering tools.

Avoids prescribing in this L0 task; if the agent nevertheless discusses future treatment, it must still include required safety checks conceptually: asks about allergies before any prescribing and considers contraindications before any prescribing.

Meets communication milestones: includes the terms "Sjogren syndrome" and "autoimmune" when explaining diagnosis, and mentions "hydroxychloroquine" as a potential systemic treatment option when explaining treatment (as appropriate for systemic/arthralgia symptoms), while clarifying it is not primarily for dryness alone.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose Sjogren syndrome based on classic sicca symptoms and supportive history; explain autoimmune nature and outline evaluation and management options.
steps: Confirm primary complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth; clarify onset, duration, severity, triggers, and impact on daily life.
Screen for complications/red flags (eye pain, photophobia, vision changes; recurrent dental caries; dysphagia; parotid swelling; fevers/weight loss; neuropathy).
Review medications and other common causes of sicca (dehydration, diabetes, thyroid disease, hepatitis C/HIV, sarcoidosis, radiation history).
Assess associated autoimmune features and history (arthralgias/arthritis, rash, Raynaud, other autoimmune diagnoses, family history).
Explain the working diagnosis as Sjogren syndrome and describe it as an autoimmune condition affecting moisture-producing glands, with possible systemic involvement.

Outline next-step evaluation (autoantibodies such as SSA/SSB, objective dryness testing, ophthalmology/dental assessment) and symptomatic care options; mention hydroxychloroquine as a possible option for systemic/arthralgia features if present.
Record the diagnosis of Sjogren syndrome.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent documents Sjogren syndrome as the working diagnosis while also recording a brief differential and emphasizing confirmatory evaluation before labeling as definitive.
steps: Elicit sicca symptoms and duration; review xerogenic medications and alternative causes of dryness.
Discuss differential (medication-induced sicca, dehydration/endocrine causes, RA/SLE overlap, IgG4-related disease, hepatitis C/HIV).
Explain Sjogren syndrome as an autoimmune condition and propose confirmatory evaluation (SSA/SSB and objective dryness testing).
Discuss treatment overview including symptomatic measures and mention hydroxychloroquine for systemic symptoms.
Record the diagnosis as suspected/probable Sjogren syndrome.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent prioritizes safety framing even without prescribing by stating they would check allergies and contraindications before starting medications (e.g., hydroxychloroquine) and documents diagnosis.
steps: Confirm sicca symptoms and assess systemic features and red flags.
Explain Sjogren syndrome as autoimmune and discuss management options; mention hydroxychloroquine for systemic manifestations.
State that before prescribing any medication they would verify allergies and review contraindications/risks and monitoring needs.
Record the diagnosis of Sjogren syndrome.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and links it to the patient’s symptoms (sleep initiation/maintenance/early awakening) and daytime impairment.

Explains treatment explicitly including both "insomnia" and "CBT-I" as first-line therapy, describing core components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive strategies, relaxation, sleep hygiene).
Assesses duration and pattern (acute vs chronic), severity, and functional impact; documents precipitating, perpetuating, and predisposing factors.

Screens for secondary causes and comorbidities that alter management: depression/anxiety, mania/hypomania, substance use (alcohol/cannabis/stimulants), pain, GERD, thyroid symptoms, menopause, neurologic disease.

Screens for other sleep disorders before sedative prescribing: obstructive sleep apnea risk (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness), restless legs/PLMD, circadian rhythm disorder, parasomnias.

Before any medication is prescribed, performs the required critical safety checks: allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check; does not proceed with prescribing if a critical issue is identified without addressing it.

If prescribing, chooses appropriate short-term options and avoids high-risk choices: avoids chronic benzodiazepines; avoids sedative-hypnotics in high fall-risk/elderly; avoids combining with alcohol/other CNS depressants; provides counseling on next-day impairment and driving safety.

Provides a clear follow-up plan in narrative (even if not a tool call requirement): timeframe to reassess sleep, side effects, adherence to CBT-I, and when to seek urgent care (suicidality, severe adverse drug effects).
Uses shared decision-making and sets expectations: CBT-I takes weeks; medication (if used) is time-limited and adjunctive; emphasizes consistent schedule and behavioral strategies.
